title: What is mental health?
citation: World Health Organization (2022). World mental health report: Transforming mental health for all. WHO.
content:
Mental health is far more than the absence of mental illness. The World Health Organization defines it as a state of wellbeing in which a person can realise their own potential, cope with the normal stresses of life, work productively and fruitfully, and contribute to their community. On this definition, mental health is not a fixed state but a dynamic capacity, something that fluctuates across the lifespan and varies with circumstances.

Mental health exists on a spectrum. At one end is flourishing, a condition in which people feel capable, connected, and purposeful. Further along the spectrum are states of distress, difficulty coping, or reduced functioning that do not meet the threshold for a clinical diagnosis but still represent a real reduction in quality of life. At the far end are severe and enduring mental health conditions that may require sustained clinical care. Most people move along this spectrum at different points in their lives, often without recognising it.

Mental health across the lifespan is shaped by a complex interplay of biological, psychological, and social factors. Genetics and early childhood experiences lay a foundation. Life events, relationships, economic security, and physical health all contribute. So does access to care, and the presence or absence of stigma in the community around a person.

Stigma remains one of the most significant barriers to mental health. When mental health difficulties are misunderstood as character weakness, lack of faith, or personal failure, people are less likely to seek help and more likely to suffer in silence. The evidence is clear that mental health conditions are common, treatable, and not a reflection of a person's character or worth.

Understanding mental health as a dimension of overall health, subject to the same kind of care and attention we would give to physical health, is the foundation for a more supportive culture.
